cuisine: Clinical Medical Nutrition — Sports and Clinical Protein Supplement
region_of_origin: Global (major producers: USA, Ireland, New Zealand); widely available in Singapore
food_subgroup: Whey protein isolate (WPI ≥90% protein by dry weight); highest biological value protein source (BV ~104); dairy-derived, essentially lactose-free

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for clinical-grade whey protein isolate powder. Closest parent concept: processed dairy protein supplement. FoodOn does not currently have a specific term for WPI ≥90% dry powder.
ontology.foodex2_description: Whey protein isolate (≥90% protein by dry weight), unflavoured dry powder — dairy-derived nutritional supplement; closest FoodEx2 parent: dairy-based protein supplement powder
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code identified for clinical-grade WPI ≥90%. Closest parent category 'Milk and milk products' (A0101) and 'Milk protein products' (A01010101) used; exact code not assigned to avoid fabrication. Code A0101010101 is for 'Whey protein concentrate'. While WPI is more refined, this is the closest available code.
ontology.snomed_ct_description: Whey protein isolate nutritional supplement (substance) — no confirmed SNOMED CT code identified; null assigned to avoid fabrication
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for whey protein isolate supplement found in publicly accessible SNOMED CT browser as of 2026.

main_ingredients: whey protein isolate (≥90% protein by dry weight)
derived from bovine milk whey — by-product of cheese manufacturing

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard clinical serving is one 30g scoop dissolved in 200–300ml of water, providing approximately 27g protein and 113 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Whey Protein Isolate — USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (Optimum Nutrition Gold Standard WPI) cross-referenced with Optimum Nutrition Singapore product label
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for Optimum Nutrition Gold Standard Whey Protein Isolate, cross-referenced with the Singapore product label. Values represent per-100g dry powder. Minor rounding differences between label and USDA entry resolved in favour of USDA FoodData Central values. This is an approved secondary global source (USDA Branded Food) appropriate for packaged/commercial protein supplement products.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of whey protein: peer-reviewed evidence — Whey protein GI ~31 based on minimal carbohydrate content; referenced in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~31 for whey protein is referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). This GI value reflects the minimal carbohydrate content of WPI (3.3g/100g; ~1g/30g serving). The glycemic load per 30g serving is approximately 0.3, which is negligible. The insulinotropic effect of whey protein is distinct from its glycemic index — whey stimulates insulin secretion independently of glucose load.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium per serving (~50mg/30g); whey-derived ACE-inhibitory bioactive peptides (lactokinins)
hypertension.guidance: Each 30g serving provides approximately 50mg sodium, which is clinically minor for most hypertensive patients; whey-derived bioactive peptides (lactokinins, beta-lactotensin) have demonstrated ACE-inhibitory activity with modest systolic blood pressure reductions of 3–6 mmHg in RCTs at 28–56g/day, supporting cautious use as a protein supplement in hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycemic load (GL ~0.3/serving); insulinotropic effect reduces postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pre-meal whey protein (20–30g, 30 minutes before eating) reduces postprandial blood glucose by approximately 30–40% in T2DM patients via leucine-stimulated insulin secretion and improved insulin sensitivity; Jakubowicz et al. 2014 (Diabetologia) demonstrated 28% lower day-long blood glucose with high-whey-protein breakfast versus isocaloric low-protein/high-carbohydrate breakfast in T2DM patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.5g/100g; 0.15g/30g serving); high-quality protein supports lean mass preservation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Whey protein isolate is very low in saturated fat (0.15g per 30g serving) and cholesterol (9mg per 30g serving), making it suitable for cardiovascular patients requiring protein supplementation; high-quality protein intake supports preservation of lean muscle mass, which is independently associated with reduced cardiovascular mortality risk in older adults.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sarcopenia risk in RA; anti-catabolic effect of leucine-rich whey protein
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients face elevated sarcopenia risk due to chronic systemic inflammation (TNF-α, IL-6-driven muscle catabolism) and glucocorticoid use; whey protein at 20–40g/day combined with resistance exercise is the most evidence-based intervention for preserving lean mass in RA, and the alpha-lactalbumin fraction provides tryptophan which may support serotonin synthesis and sleep quality relevant to RA pain management.

lactose_intolerance.guidance: WPI contains less than 0.1g lactose per 30g serving, making it suitable for lactose-intolerant patients who require dairy-derived protein supplementation, in contrast to whey protein concentrate (WPC) which contains 3–5g lactose per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Whey protein isolate (WPI), unflavoured clinical grade, is a highly purified dairy-derived protein powder containing ≥90% protein by dry weight, produced by ultrafiltration and ion exchange chromatography of bovine milk whey — the liquid by-product of cheese manufacturing. Per 100g dry powder: 376 kcal, 90g protein, 1.6g fat (0.5g saturated), 3.3g carbohydrate (3.0g sugar), 0g dietary fibre, 165mg sodium. Per standard 30g clinical scoop: approximately 27g protein, 113 kcal, 50mg sodium, 0.5g fat, 1g carbohydrate. WPI has the highest biological value (BV ~104) of any common protein source and provides approximately 2.8g leucine per 30g serving — at or above the mTORC1 activation threshold for maximal muscle protein synthesis. WPI is essentially lactose-free (<0.1g/30g serving), distinguishing it from whey protein concentrate (WPC, 3–5g lactose/serving) and making it suitable for lactose-intolerant patients. Taste: neutral, slightly milky when dissolved; mixes readily in water. Clinical applications include post-surgical protein repletion, sarcopenia management, malnutrition rehabilitation, and pre-meal glucose attenuation in T2DM. Pre-meal whey protein (20–30g, 30 min before eating) reduces postprandial glucose by ~30–40% via leucine-stimulated insulin secretion. GI ~31 (low); glycemic load per 30g serving ~0.3 (negligible). Available in Singapore at GNC, Guardian, Lazada, Shopee; clinical-grade versions (e.g., Beneprotein by Nestlé Health Science) through hospital pharmacies. Major global producers: Agropur and Hilmar Cheese Company (USA), Kerry Group (Ireland), Fonterra (New Zealand).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (approved secondary global source for packaged/commercial products), cross-referenced with Optimum Nutrition Singapore product label. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the verified source. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Confidence is high given the availability of a directly applicable branded food entry in an approved source.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — WPI dry powder contains no dietary fibre as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central branded entry. vitamin_c_mg and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central branded entry for this product formulation. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per product label and USDA FoodData Central entry. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data not reported in the USDA FoodData Central branded entry for this WPI product. water_g set to -1: moisture content of dry powder not reported in the USDA FoodData Central branded entry consulted. GI ~31 reflects the minimal carbohydrate content of WPI (3.3g/100g); the insulinotropic effect of whey protein is a distinct pharmacological mechanism independent of glycemic index. This record represents a generic clinical-grade WPI ≥90% protein; specific branded products may vary slightly in micronutrient content. Ontology: no exact FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, or SNOMED CT code identified for clinical-grade WPI ≥90% dry powder; closest parent categories used with gap notes to avoid fabrication. Multilingual note: Tamil and Hindi local names and aliases added as per G6 requirements for Singaporean context, even though it's a global supplement, as it's widely used in Singapore.
